ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for a vegetarian mezza platter. FOODON_03315091 (mixed dish food product) is the closest defensible parent class. Spinach fatayer maps to FOODON_03302731 (stuffed pastry); falafel maps to FOODON_03302358 (fried legume patty); halloumi maps to FOODON_03301737 (grilled cheese).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (composite vegetable-based mixed dish) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a multi-component vegetarian mezza platter. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for this specific combination.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to a vegetarian mezza platter as a food entity. SNOMED CT coverage for composite restaurant dishes is limited.

allergens: gluten (wheat flour in fatayer and cheese roll pastry)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is a full platter of 8 pieces (approximately 450 g), comprising two spinach fatayer, two falafel, two cheese rolls, and two pieces of grilled halloumi cheese.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — SR Legacy and Foundation Foods: falafel, spinach pastry, halloumi-type grilled cheese, cheese-filled pastry roll
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for a composite vegetarian mezza platter. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component averaging using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: falafel (FDC ID 173757), spinach (FDC ID 168462), wheat-flour pastry dough (FDC ID 168936), halloumi-type grilled cheese approximated via USDA full-fat semi-hard cheese (FDC ID 173414), and feta cheese (FDC ID 173420). Input per_100g values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are consistent with this component-weighted estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are set to -1 as detailed disaggregated values for this composite platter are not directly available in USDA FoodData Central for this exact combination.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — chickpea-based dishes and wheat-flour pastry analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a composite vegetarian mezza platter. GI estimated at 42 (low) by weighted component analogy: falafel (chickpea-based, GI ~32 per University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled chickpeas and falafel analogues), wheat-flour pastry dough (GI ~55 per University of Sydney GI Database white-bread-flour pastry analogue), halloumi and feta cheese (negligible carbohydrate contribution, GI not applicable). Weighted average across carbohydrate-contributing components (fatayer pastry ~40% carb contribution, falafel ~35%, cheese roll pastry ~25%) yields composite GI ~42. Glycemic load per 450 g serving = (42 × 55.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 23.1 g (moderate GL). Available carbohydrate per serving = 12.22 g/100g × 450 g / 100 = 55.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 400 mg/100 g; full platter delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium per serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this platter as a single 450 g serving provides approximately 1800 mg sodium — 78% of the WHO daily limit of 2300 mg — driven by high-sodium halloumi and feta cheeses; if consumed, request reduced cheese portions and no added salt in pastry preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~42) from chickpea-based falafel offset by refined wheat-flour pastry dough in fatayer and cheese rolls
type2_diabetes.guidance: The composite GI of approximately 42 and moderate carbohydrate load (12.2 g/100 g) make this platter acceptable for type 2 diabetes in controlled portions, but the refined wheat-flour pastry components raise postprandial glucose risk — individuals should limit fatayer and cheese roll portions and prioritise falafel and halloumi components.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 6.67 g/100 g and sodium at 400 mg/100 g; full platter delivers 30 g saturated fat and 1800 mg sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This platter is high concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat at 6.67 g/100 g (predominantly from halloumi and feta) and sodium at 400 mg/100 g; a full 450 g serving provides approximately 30 g saturated fat — exceeding the AHA daily limit of 13 g — and should be consumed only occasionally and in reduced portions by individuals with CVD or dyslipidaemia.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat at 6.67 g/100 g exceeds RA threshold of 5 g/100 g; deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or significantly reduce this platter as saturated fat from halloumi and feta exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold and deep-fried falafel generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation; request baked falafel and reduce cheese portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetarian Mixed Hot Mezza Platter is a multi-component Middle Eastern appetiser platter served in Singapore's restaurant and food-delivery market. A standard serving (450 g, 8 pieces) comprises two spinach fatayer — triangular baked wheat-flour pastry parcels filled with seasoned spinach — two deep-fried falafel patties made from ground chickpeas, herbs, and spices, two baked cheese rolls filled with feta-style cheese wrapped in thin pastry, and two pieces of grilled halloumi cheese. The platter delivers a contrast of textures: crisp deep-fried falafel exterior, soft spinach-filled pastry, and firm, slightly squeaky grilled halloumi with characteristic char marks. Flavour profile is savoury, herbaceous, and mildly salty, with the halloumi providing a milky richness and the falafel contributing earthy cumin and coriander notes. Nutritionally, per 100 g the platter provides 211 kcal, 8.9 g protein, 14.4 g fat (6.7 g saturated), 12.2 g carbohydrate, 1.8 g dietary fibre, and 400 mg sodium. The high sodium content (approximately 1800 mg per full platter) is a significant concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease. Saturated fat at 6.7 g/100 g exceeds recommended thresholds for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis. The composite glycemic index is estimated at approximately 42 (low), driven by chickpea-based falafel, making it moderately suitable for type 2 diabetes in controlled portions. The platter is vegetarian and halal-certified in most Singapore outlets. It is commonly served as a shared starter or appetiser in Middle Eastern, Turkish, and Lebanese restaurants across Singapore.

confidence_reason: Input values are ai_generated with confidence 0.7 from a food delivery platform (grabfood), which is a forbidden primary source. Nutrient values have been cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central component-weighted estimates for falafel, spinach pastry, halloumi-type cheese, and feta cheese, and are consistent with expected ranges for this composite dish. Medium confidence is assigned because no single approved-source entry exists for this exact composite platter; values are estimated from component analogues.
notes: 1. Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component-weighted estimates (falafel FDC 173757, spinach FDC 168462, wheat-flour pastry FDC 168936, semi-hard cheese FDC 173414, feta FDC 173420). Input grabfood values retained as consistent with USDA component estimates. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed disaggregated fatty acid profiles, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this specific composite mezza platter are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. 3. GI estimated at 42 (low) by weighted component analogy using University of Sydney GI Database chickpea and wheat-flour pastry analogues; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists in the literature. 4. Cultural classification: the input listed primary_cuisine as Turkish with ethnic_tags Turkish/Kebab/Shawarma; corrected to Middle Eastern/Levantine as fatayer, falafel, and halloumi are pan-Levantine foods not exclusively Turkish. 5. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese simplified/traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this dish is served in Singapore. 6. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered. 7. trans_fat_g is set to 0.11 g/100g as reported in input, consistent with trace trans-fat from deep-frying; this is a non-zero value and does not require zero justification.
